summary: Gruber reflects on the 2024 election results through the lens of Hunter S. Thompson's 1972 writing about Nixon's landslide reelection, drawing parallels between the despair of that era and the present. He revisits his own 2008 post celebrating Obama's victory, where he optimistically declared 'it ends here, today,' now acknowledging that sentiment as naive. He contextualizes Trump's 2024 win by noting it was far smaller than both Obama's victories and Nixon's 1972 landslide, arguing the situation is bad but not unprecedented. The post concludes that progress is real but never permanent, always met with backlash, and urges readers to keep perspective and persevere.
key_points: Thompson's 1972 despair about Nixon's America reads as painfully relevant to the present moment
Obama's 2008 and 2012 victories were both larger than Trump's 2024 win by Electoral College and popular vote margins
The 2008 Obama victory represented a high-water mark that made Gruber's optimism feel justified at the time but naive in retrospect
Trump's win, while 'bad and dangerous and dark,' was still a close, deeply divided election — not a Nixon-style landslide mandate
Progress is real (Obama ran opposing gay marriage just 16 years ago) but never linear and never without backlash
money_quotes: If every damn word of that doesn't ring true to you today, you're deaf.
That first sentence sounds hopelessly naive today. I don't blame my younger self for having written it though.
That 2008 Obama win was euphoric. It remains our nation's high-water mark.
The lesson is that it never ends.
This is bad and dangerous and dark, but while Trump's win was brutally clear, it was still a very close, deeply divided election.
Barack Obama ran in 2008 opposing gay marriage. The Democrat. That was only 16 years ago. The iPhone was already out.
Progress hasn't stopped, but it's never easy, and never without backlash.
It doesn't end. Keep the faith.
key_insight: American political progress is real but cyclical — despair and hope trade places across decades, and neither landslide victories nor devastating losses are ever the final word.
